title: Training Stability: VAE vs. GAN
concept: VAEs train much more stably than GANs (callback to Units 205-208's GAN training struggles), since there's no adversarial competition to balance.

exercises.prompt: Quick refresh: In a GAN, what two networks are constantly battling each other?
exercises.explain: Right! GANs have a Generator trying to fool a Discriminator, and that back-and-forth battle is what makes GANs tricky to train.

cards.title: Remember the GAN Roller Coaster?
cards.body: Back in Units 205-208, training a GAN felt like a wild roller coaster — the generator and discriminator fought each other, and training could crash or freeze. What if there was a model that trained smoothly, like a calm walk instead of a roller coaster? Meet the VAE.

cards.title: Training Stability
cards.body: VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) train much more stably than GANs. Why? A VAE has just ONE goal: recreate its input well and keep its hidden space organized. There's no second network fighting against it, so the training process moves smoothly toward that one goal instead of two competing goals pulling in different directions.

cards.title: Solo Hike vs. Tug-of-War
cards.body: Training a VAE is like going on a solo hike — you follow one clear path at your own steady pace. Training a GAN is like a tug-of-war — two teams pulling against each other, and if one team suddenly gets too strong, the rope (training) can snap or go haywire.

cards.title: Watching the Loss Curve
cards.body: If you plot a VAE's loss during training, it usually goes down smoothly and steadily, like a gentle slide. A GAN's loss curves often bounce up and down unpredictably because the generator and discriminator are constantly reacting to each other's improvements.

cards.title: Myth: Stable Training Means Better Images
cards.body: Myth: If VAEs train more stably, they must make better images than GANs. Truth: Stability just means the TRAINING PROCESS is smoother and more predictable. GANs can actually produce sharper, more realistic images once they DO train successfully — they're just harder to get there!

cards.title: Two Training Journeys
cards.body: Compare how each model's training feels from start to finish.

left.text: One network, one loss, smooth and steady progress with fewer surprises.

right.text: Two networks competing, loss can spike, and training can collapse if one side wins too fast.

exercises.prompt: Why do VAEs generally train more stably than GANs?

options.text: VAEs don't have an adversarial competition between two networks
VAEs don't use neural networks at all
VAEs only work on small images

exercises.explain: Correct! Without two networks fighting each other, a VAE just optimizes one smooth goal, avoiding the instability that comes from adversarial competition.

exercises.prompt: True or False: GAN training can become unstable because the generator and discriminator are constantly competing against each other.
exercises.explain: True! This back-and-forth competition is exactly why GANs can be trickier to train than VAEs.

exercises.sentence: VAEs train more stably than GANs because there is no ______ competition to balance.
exercises.explain: ‘Adversarial’ describes the fight between GAN's generator and discriminator — VAEs skip that fight entirely.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best describes a stable training process?

options.text: The loss steadily decreases over time without wild swings
The loss jumps unpredictably and the model sometimes stops improving
Two networks constantly outsmart each other, causing sudden crashes

exercises.explain: Stable training looks like smooth, steady improvement — the calm hike, not the tug-of-war.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'GANs are always worse than VAEs because GAN training is unstable.' What's the best way to correct this statement?

options.text: Explain that VAEs train more smoothly, but GANs can produce sharper results once trained successfully — stability and output quality are different things
Agree completely — GANs are objectively worse in every way
Say that GANs don't actually have two networks, so this isn't true
Say training stability has nothing to do with generator vs discriminator competition

exercises.explain: The key insight: training stability (how smoothly a model learns) is separate from output quality (how good the results look). VAEs win on stability; GANs can win on sharpness — it's a trade-off, not one being 'better' overall.
